steps.title: Meet the Circle
steps.goal: See how the center and radius define a circle.
steps.introText: A circle is the set of all points in a plane that are the same distance r from a fixed point called the center.
steps.prompt: Adjust the center and radius to change the circle dimensions and position. Drag the black point around the circle to see how the distance from any point on the circle to the center stays the same.

steps.title: The Equation of a Circle
steps.goal: Understand the equation of a circle.
steps.introText: Given a circle whose center is at (h, k) and that has a radius of r, its equation can be expressed in the form **(x − h)² + (y − k)² = r²**. This is derived from the distance formula.
steps.prompt: Try at least three different combinations of h, k, and r. Watch how the center, radius, and equation change each time.

steps.title: Match the Equation
steps.goal: Now that we learned how the equation of a circle is derived, see if you can work backwards to graph a circle given its equation.
steps.prompt: Create a circle with equation x² + y² = 49.

feedback.correct: Perfect! With r = 7, the equation is x² + y² = 49.
feedback.incorrect: The radius is not quite right yet, keep adjusting.
feedback.hint: In this equation, r² = 49, so r = 7. So, you should set the radius to 7.

steps.title: Center and Radius
steps.goal: Reiterate what h, k, and r represent in the standard equation.
steps.prompt: A circle has center (3, −2) and radius 4. Which equation is correct?
steps.feedback: Exactly right, nice job translating the information into equation form.
steps.incorrectFeedback: Not quite. Go back to a previous step if you are having trouble remembering what h, k, and r represent in the standard equation.

steps.title: Shifted Circle
steps.goal: Graph a circle without knowing its equation, only given its center and radius.
steps.prompt: Graph a circle whose center is at (3, −2) and radius is 5.

feedback.correct: Nice! Double check that the correct equation (x − 3)² + (y + 2)² = 25 makes sense to you.
feedback.incorrect: Double check your center and radius and try again.
feedback.hint: Move the red center handle to (3, −2), then set r = 5 with the orange handle.

steps.title: Small Shifted Circle
steps.goal: Practice some more with graphing circles.
steps.prompt: Graph a small circle with center (−2, 1). We will consider any circle with r ≤ 1.5 small.

feedback.correct: Well done! A small r gives a tight circle even when the center is shifted.
feedback.incorrect: Double check your center and radius and try again.
feedback.hint: Set the center first, then drag the orange handle inward.

steps.title: Check Your Understanding
steps.prompt: Which statement is always true for a circle in standard form?

choices.label: The center is at the origin.
r appears on the right side of the equation.
Every point on the circle is distance r from the center (h, k).
h and k are always positive.

steps.feedback: Nice job remembering! The center and radius together define every point on the circle.
steps.incorrectFeedback: Not quite. Try to recall what the core definition of a circle is.

steps.title: Mastery Check
steps.goal: Construct four circles with specific size and position — no hints.
steps.completionMessage: Congratulations! You now understand the basics of circles. Next, we'll look at ellipses and see how they are similar yet different compared to circles.
